lines.raw_text: **Yes, untethered underwater robots known as Autonomous Underwater Vehicles (AUVs) exist and can perform ship hull inspections without tethers or large setups, operating independently using onboard sensors for navigation and data collection.**[1][3][4][7]
lines.tagged_text: <null>**Yes, untethered underwater robots known as Autonomous Underwater Vehicles (AUVs) exist and can perform ship hull inspections without tethers or large setups, operating independently using onboard sensors for navigation and data collection.**[1][3][4][7]</null>

spans.text: **Yes, untethered underwater robots known as Autonomous Underwater Vehicles (AUVs) exist and can perform ship hull inspections without tethers or large setups, operating independently using onboard sensors for navigation and data collection.**[1][3][4][7]

lines.raw_text: ### Key Examples and Capabilities
lines.tagged_text: <null>### Key Examples and Capabilities</null>

lines.raw_text: These AUVs use technologies like sonar, cameras, and SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping) for autonomous operation, scanning hulls for corrosion, biofouling, damage, and 3D mapping without human divers or surface support.[1][3][4]
lines.tagged_text: <null>These AUVs use technologies like sonar, cameras, and SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping) for autonomous operation, scanning hulls for corrosion, biofouling, damage, and 3D mapping without human divers or surface support.[1][3][4]</null>

spans.text: These AUVs use technologies like sonar, cameras, and SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping) for autonomous operation, scanning hulls for corrosion, biofouling, damage, and 3D mapping without human divers or surface support.[1][3][4]

lines.raw_text: - **IQUA Robotics' Sparus II AUV**: Proven in real-world tests for fully autonomous hull scanning with multibeam sonar, maintaining constant distance from the hull and generating centimeter-resolution 3D models to detect issues like marine growth and paint damage. No tether or diver needed.[1]
lines.tagged_text: <null>- **IQUA Robotics' Sparus II AUV**: Proven in real-world tests for fully autonomous hull scanning with multibeam sonar, maintaining constant distance from the hull and generating centimeter-resolution 3D models to detect issues like marine growth and paint damage. No tether or diver needed.[1]</null>

spans.text: - **IQUA Robotics' Sparus II AUV**: Proven in real-world tests for fully autonomous hull scanning with multibeam sonar, maintaining constant distance from the hull and generating centimeter-resolution 3D models to detect issues like marine growth and paint damage. No tether or diver needed.[1]

lines.raw_text: - **Bluefin Robotics HAUV**: A hovering AUV used for extended hull inspections (e.g., 3.4-hour missions covering 2.7 km), employing real-time visual SLAM for navigation in feature-poor underwater environments. Designed for precise standoff distances (around 1 meter) with cameras and sonar.[4]
lines.tagged_text: <null>- **Bluefin Robotics HAUV**: A hovering AUV used for extended hull inspections (e.g., 3.4-hour missions covering 2.7 km), employing real-time visual SLAM for navigation in feature-poor underwater environments. Designed for precise standoff distances (around 1 meter) with cameras and sonar.[4]</null>

spans.text: - **Bluefin Robotics HAUV**: A hovering AUV used for extended hull inspections (e.g., 3.4-hour missions covering 2.7 km), employing real-time visual SLAM for navigation in feature-poor underwater environments. Designed for precise standoff distances (around 1 meter) with cameras and sonar.[4]

lines.raw_text: - **Hover-capable AUV systems**: Research demonstrates stereo cameras for surface normal estimation, enabling navigation over curved hulls and robust visual mapping in real-sea conditions on full-scale ships.[3]
lines.tagged_text: <null>- **Hover-capable AUV systems**: Research demonstrates stereo cameras for surface normal estimation, enabling navigation over curved hulls and robust visual mapping in real-sea conditions on full-scale ships.[3]</null>

spans.text: - **Hover-capable AUV systems**: Research demonstrates stereo cameras for surface normal estimation, enabling navigation over curved hulls and robust visual mapping in real-sea conditions on full-scale ships.[3]

lines.raw_text: In contrast, most Remotely Operated Vehicles (ROVs) like Blueye X3 or Deep Trekker crawlers require tethers for control and power, though some (e.g., Hydromea's EXRAY with "flyout" mode) offer limited untethered extensions from a tethered "mothership."[2][5][6][7] True AUVs eliminate this entirely, operating independently like uncrewed survey tools.[7]
lines.tagged_text: <null>In contrast, most Remotely Operated Vehicles (ROVs) like Blueye X3 or Deep Trekker crawlers require tethers for control and power, though some (e.g., Hydromea's EXRAY with "flyout" mode) offer limited untethered extensions from a tethered "mothership."[2][5][6][7] True AUVs eliminate this entirely, operating independently like uncrewed survey tools.[7]</null>

spans.text: In contrast, most Remotely Operated Vehicles (ROVs) like Blueye X3 or Deep Trekker crawlers require tethers for control and power, though some (e.g., Hydromea's EXRAY with "flyout" mode) offer limited untethered extensions from a tethered "mothership."[2][5][6][7] True AUVs eliminate this entirely, operating independently like uncrewed survey tools.[7]

lines.raw_text: ### Advantages Over Tethered or Diver-Based Methods
lines.tagged_text: <null>### Advantages Over Tethered or Diver-Based Methods</null>

spans.text: ### Advantages Over Tethered or Diver-Based Methods

lines.raw_text: - **No big setup**: Deployable from small vessels; self-contained batteries and autonomy reduce logistics.[1][4][7]
lines.tagged_text: <null>- **No big setup**: Deployable from small vessels; self-contained batteries and autonomy reduce logistics.[1][4][7]</null>

spans.text: - **No big setup**: Deployable from small vessels; self-contained batteries and autonomy reduce logistics.[1][4][7]

lines.raw_text: - **Safety and efficiency**: Avoids diver risks and enables frequent inspections for fuel savings, compliance, and biosecurity.[1][2]
lines.tagged_text: <null>- **Safety and efficiency**: Avoids diver risks and enables frequent inspections for fuel savings, compliance, and biosecurity.[1][2]</null>

spans.text: - **Safety and efficiency**: Avoids diver risks and enables frequent inspections for fuel savings, compliance, and biosecurity.[1][2]

lines.raw_text: - **Limitations**: Best for visual/sonar surveys; complex environments may need hybrid approaches, and battery life limits mission duration (hours, not days).[3][4]
lines.tagged_text: <null>- **Limitations**: Best for visual/sonar surveys; complex environments may need hybrid approaches, and battery life limits mission duration (hours, not days).[3][4]</null>

spans.text: - **Limitations**: Best for visual/sonar surveys; complex environments may need hybrid approaches, and battery life limits mission duration (hours, not days).[3][4]

lines.raw_text: **Creating an underwater Wi-Fi-like network for robots requires optical or acoustic communication technologies, as radio waves like traditional Wi-Fi don't propagate well in water.**[1][4][7] These alternatives enable data exchange among robots, sensors, and surface stations, with optical methods offering higher speeds for short ranges and acoustics providing longer-range reliability at lower bandwidths.[3][5]
lines.tagged_text: <null>**Creating an underwater Wi-Fi-like network for robots requires optical or acoustic communication technologies, as radio waves like traditional Wi-Fi don't propagate well in water.**[1][4][7] These alternatives enable data exchange among robots, sensors, and surface stations, with optical methods offering higher speeds for short ranges and acoustics providing longer-range reliability at lower bandwidths.[3][5]</null>

spans.text: **Creating an underwater Wi-Fi-like network for robots requires optical or acoustic communication technologies, as radio waves like traditional Wi-Fi don't propagate well in water.**[1][4][7] These alternatives enable data exchange among robots, sensors, and surface stations, with optical methods offering higher speeds for short ranges and acoustics providing longer-range reliability at lower bandwidths.[3][5]

lines.raw_text: ### Key Technologies and How They Work
lines.tagged_text: <null>### Key Technologies and How They Work</null>

spans.text: ### Key Technologies and How They Work

lines.raw_text: Several proven systems mimic Wi-Fi functionality underwater by networking multiple devices:
lines.tagged_text: <null>Several proven systems mimic Wi-Fi functionality underwater by networking multiple devices:</null>

spans.text: Several proven systems mimic Wi-Fi functionality underwater by networking multiple devices:

lines.raw_text: - **Optical Communication (Light-Based, High-Speed "Underwater Wi-Fi")**:
lines.tagged_text: <null>- **Optical Communication (Light-Based, High-Speed "Underwater Wi-Fi")**:</null>

lines.raw_text: - Uses modulated LED or laser light (e.g., blue/green/UV wavelengths) to transmit data via line-of-sight beams.[1][2][5]
lines.tagged_text: <null>  - Uses modulated LED or laser light (e.g., blue/green/UV wavelengths) to transmit data via line-of-sight beams.[1][2][5]</null>

spans.text: - Uses modulated LED or laser light (e.g., blue/green/UV wavelengths) to transmit data via line-of-sight beams.[1][2][5]

lines.raw_text: - **Hydromea's LUMA**: Compact modems deliver up to **10 Mbps** for HD video streaming, working from splash zones to 6,000m depths over 50m ranges. Robots equip modems as access points; data converts to light pulses (e.g., 470nm LEDs) for real-time control and video between robots or to surfaces.[1][2]

lines.raw_text: - **Aqua-Fi**: Green LEDs (520nm) or lasers achieve 2.11 MB/s transfers for multimedia; pair with surface gateways using radio for internet uplink. Ideal for robot swarms in clear water, but needs alignment—future spherical receivers could enable omni-directional networking.[5]
lines.tagged_text: - **<BrandPositioning>Aqua-Fi</BrandPositioning><null>**: Green LEDs (</null><StatisticalUse>520nm</StatisticalUse><null>) or lasers achieve </null><StatisticalUse>2.11 MB/s</StatisticalUse><null> transfers for multimedia; pair with surface gateways using radio for internet uplink. </null><StrongRecommendation>Ideal for robot swarms</StrongRecommendation><null> in clear water, but needs alignment—future spherical receivers could enable omni-directional networking.[5]</null>

lines.raw_text: - **Implementation Steps**:
lines.tagged_text: <null>  - **Implementation Steps**:</null>

lines.raw_text: 1. Install modems on each robot (pressure-sealed, e.g., molded plastic).[2]
lines.tagged_text: <null>    1. Install modems on each robot (pressure-sealed, e.g., molded plastic).[2]</null>

spans.text: 1. Install modems on each robot (pressure-sealed, e.g., molded plastic).[2]

lines.raw_text: 2. Network via mesh topology: Robots relay light signals point-to-point.
lines.tagged_text: <null>    2. Network via mesh topology: Robots relay light signals point-to-point.</null>

spans.text: 2. Network via mesh topology: Robots relay light signals point-to-point.

lines.raw_text: 3. Handle turbidity with UV light (less affected by ambient sources).[1]
lines.tagged_text: <null>    3. Handle turbidity with UV light (less affected by ambient sources).[1]</null>

spans.text: 3. Handle turbidity with UV light (less affected by ambient sources).[1]

lines.raw_text: - **Acoustic Communication (Sound-Based, Long-Range)**:
lines.tagged_text: <null>- **Acoustic Communication (Sound-Based, Long-Range)**:</null>

lines.raw_text: - Employs sound waves for data muling, suitable for murky water or km-scale networks where optics fail.[4][6][7]
lines.tagged_text: <null>  - Employs sound waves for data muling, suitable for murky water or </null><StatisticalUse>km-scale</StatisticalUse><null> networks where optics fail.[4][6][7]</null>

lines.raw_text: ### Steps to Build Your Network
lines.tagged_text: <null>### Steps to Build Your Network</null>

lines.raw_text: 1. **Assess Environment**: Test water clarity/depth—optics for shallow/clear, acoustics for deep/turbid.[1][7]
lines.tagged_text: <null>1. **Assess Environment**: Test water clarity/depth—optics for shallow/clear, acoustics for deep/turbid.[1][7]</null>

spans.text: 1. **Assess Environment**: Test water clarity/depth—optics for shallow/clear, acoustics for deep/turbid.[1][7]

lines.raw_text: 2. **Select Hardware**: Use commercial modems like [Hydromea LUMA](https://www.hydromea.com/) or WSense; integrate via APIs for robot control.[1][3]
lines.tagged_text: <null>2. **Select Hardware**: Use commercial modems like [</null><BrandPositioning>Hydromea LUMA</BrandPositioning><null>](https://www.hydromea.com/) or </null><BrandPositioning>WSense</BrandPositioning><null>; integrate via APIs for robot control.[1][3]</null>

spans.text: ; integrate via APIs for robot control.[1][3]

lines.raw_text: 3. **Network Design**: Deploy mesh (robots relay data) or star (central gateway); add SDN for management.[6]
lines.tagged_text: <null>3. **Network Design**: Deploy mesh (robots relay data) or star (central gateway); add SDN for management.[6]</null>

spans.text: 3. **Network Design**: Deploy mesh (robots relay data) or star (central gateway); add SDN for management.[6]

lines.raw_text: 4. **Power & Integration**: Opt for efficient, pressure-rated units; test in pools before open water.[2]
lines.tagged_text: <null>4. **Power & Integration**: Opt for efficient, pressure-rated units; test in pools before open water.[2]</null>

spans.text: 4. **Power & Integration**: Opt for efficient, pressure-rated units; test in pools before open water.[2]

lines.raw_text: 5. **Hybrid Approach**: Optics for local robot-robot links, acoustics for surface uplink.[6]
lines.tagged_text: <null>5. **Hybrid Approach**: Optics for local robot-robot links, acoustics for surface uplink.[6]</null>

spans.text: 5. **Hybrid Approach**: Optics for local robot-robot links, acoustics for surface uplink.[6]

lines.raw_text: 6. **Prototyping**: Start small—e.g., 2-3 robots exchanging sensor data—scaling with simulations.[6]

lines.raw_text: **Underwater Wi-Fi using LED-based optical signals, known as LED-based underwater wireless optical communication (UWOC), is in an advanced experimental stage with real-time demonstrations achieving data rates up to 135 Mbps over 10 meters in controlled pool tests and potential for longer ranges in research setups.[1][4]**
lines.tagged_text: <null>**Underwater Wi-Fi using LED-based optical signals, known as LED-based underwater wireless optical communication (UWOC), is in an advanced experimental stage with real-time demonstrations achieving </null><StatisticalUse>data rates up to 135 Mbps</StatisticalUse><null> over </null><StatisticalUse>10 meters</StatisticalUse><null> in controlled pool tests and potential for longer ranges in research setups.[1][4]**</null>

spans.text: **Underwater Wi-Fi using LED-based optical signals, known as LED-based underwater wireless optical communication (UWOC), is in an advanced experimental stage with real-time demonstrations achieving
in controlled pool tests and potential for longer ranges in research setups.[1][4]**

lines.raw_text: ### Key Achievements and Technologies
lines.tagged_text: <null>### Key Achievements and Technologies</null>

lines.raw_text: LED-based UWOC leverages blue or green LEDs (typically 400-500 nm wavelengths for minimal seawater attenuation) to transmit data via modulated light, offering higher speeds and lower latency than acoustic alternatives.[1][3][4]
lines.tagged_text: <null>LED-based UWOC leverages blue or green LEDs (typically </null><StatisticalUse>400-500 nm</StatisticalUse><null> wavelengths for minimal seawater attenuation) to transmit data via modulated light, offering higher speeds and lower latency than acoustic alternatives.[1][3][4]</null>

spans.text: LED-based UWOC leverages blue or green LEDs (typically
wavelengths for minimal seawater attenuation) to transmit data via modulated light, offering higher speeds and lower latency than acoustic alternatives.[1][3][4]

lines.raw_text: - A 2023 real-time system used four high-power blue LEDs (OSRAM GD CS8PM1.14, 451 nm peak) with pre-emphasis to extend bandwidth to 40.3 MHz, paired with a 3 mm APD receiver, achieving **135 Mbps** at 10 m in a pool with BER of 5.9 × 10⁻³ and water attenuation of 0.289 dB/m.[1]
lines.tagged_text: <null>- A </null><StatisticalUse>2023</StatisticalUse><null> real-time system used </null><StatisticalUse>four</StatisticalUse><null> high-power blue LEDs (</null><BrandPositioning>OSRAM GD CS8PM1.14</BrandPositioning><null>, </null><StatisticalUse>451 nm</StatisticalUse><null> peak) with pre-emphasis to extend bandwidth to </null><StatisticalUse>40.3 MHz</StatisticalUse><null>, paired with a </null><StatisticalUse>3 mm</StatisticalUse><null> APD receiver, achieving **</null><StatisticalUse>135 Mbps</StatisticalUse><null>** at </null><StatisticalUse>10 m</StatisticalUse><null> in a pool with BER of </null><StatisticalUse>5.9 × 10⁻³</StatisticalUse><null> and water attenuation of </null><StatisticalUse>0.289 dB/m</StatisticalUse><null>.[1]</null>

lines.raw_text: - Deep-sea tests at 1650 m depth reached **125 Mbps** over 30 m with direction-adjustable green LEDs (max distance 117.7 m at that rate) and 6.25 Mbps NLOS blue light, enabling lossless 8K 30 FPS and 4K 120 FPS video over 30 days with BER < 10⁻⁵.[4]

lines.raw_text: ### Challenges and Limitations
lines.tagged_text: <null>### Challenges and Limitations</null>

lines.raw_text: - **Range and environment**: Limited to tens of meters in turbid water due to absorption/scattering; pure seawater favors blue-green bands, but real oceans reduce performance.[3][4]
lines.tagged_text: <null>- **Range and environment**: Limited to </null><StatisticalUse>tens of meters</StatisticalUse><null> in turbid water due to absorption/scattering; pure seawater favors blue-green bands, but real oceans reduce performance.[3][4]</null>

spans.text: in turbid water due to absorption/scattering; pure seawater favors blue-green bands, but real oceans reduce performance.[3][4]

lines.raw_text: - **Not yet commercial "Wi-Fi"**: Primarily research prototypes for applications like seafloor observation, environmental monitoring, and ROV networking—no widespread deployment as of latest reports.[1][3][4]
lines.tagged_text: <null>- **Not yet commercial "Wi-Fi"**: Primarily research prototypes for applications like seafloor observation, environmental monitoring, and ROV networking—no widespread deployment as of latest reports.[1][3][4]</null>

spans.text: - **Not yet commercial "Wi-Fi"**: Primarily research prototypes for applications like seafloor observation, environmental monitoring, and ROV networking—no widespread deployment as of latest reports.[1][3][4]

lines.raw_text: - Laser diodes outperform LEDs for longer ranges (e.g., 105 m at depth >1000 m), but LEDs enable cost-effective, wider-angle links.[2]
lines.tagged_text: <null>- Laser diodes outperform LEDs for longer ranges (e.g., </null><StatisticalUse>105 m</StatisticalUse><null> at depth </null><StatisticalUse>>1000 m</StatisticalUse><null>), but LEDs enable cost-effective, wider-angle links.[2]</null>

lines.raw_text: ### Current Development Status
lines.tagged_text: <null>### Current Development Status</null>

lines.raw_text: Research is rapidly advancing toward reliable, high-speed links for deep-sea networks, with long-term stability proven in field trials.[4] Most projects target the 400-500 nm band for optimal propagation.[3] No evidence of consumer "underwater Wi-Fi" products, but it's promising for marine tech.

lines.tagged_text: <null>### </null><BrandPositioning>Copenhagen Subsea</BrandPositioning><null> Thrusters</null>

lines.raw_text: These electric thrusters feature a rim-driven design with only one moving part, no gears, no oil, and seawater-lubricated bearings, enabling high maneuverability and virtually silent operation ideal for confined environments like marine protected areas.[1]

lines.raw_text: **Underwater inspection tools for industrial basins and flooded structures primarily include ultrasonic thickness gauges, ROVs (Remotely Operated Vehicles), video cameras, and non-destructive testing (NDT) systems like corrosion monitors and ACFM probes.** These tools enable visual checks, thickness measurements, corrosion detection, and structural assessments in submerged environments such as caissons, dams, bridges, locks, and wastewater facilities[1][2][3][5].
lines.tagged_text: <null>**Underwater inspection tools for industrial basins and flooded structures primarily include ultrasonic thickness gauges, ROVs (Remotely Operated Vehicles), video cameras, and non-destructive testing (NDT) systems like corrosion monitors and ACFM probes.** These tools enable visual checks, thickness measurements, corrosion detection, and structural assessments in submerged environments such as caissons, dams, bridges, locks, and wastewater facilities[1][2][3][5].</null>

spans.text: **Underwater inspection tools for industrial basins and flooded structures primarily include ultrasonic thickness gauges, ROVs (Remotely Operated Vehicles), video cameras, and non-destructive testing (NDT) systems like corrosion monitors and ACFM probes.** These tools enable visual checks, thickness measurements, corrosion detection, and structural assessments in submerged environments such as caissons, dams, bridges, locks, and wastewater facilities[1][2][3][5].

lines.raw_text: ### Key Tools and Their Applications
lines.tagged_text: <null>### Key Tools and Their Applications</null>

lines.raw_text: - **Ultrasonic Thickness Gauges (e.g., Cygnus Instruments)**: Diver-held, wrist-mounted (Cygnus DIVE), or ROV-mountable models (up to 3,000m depth) measure metal thickness on bridges, caissons, canal locks, jetties, piers, pilings, and subsea structures. Proven reliable for over 20 years in hostile conditions[1].

lines.raw_text: - Employs sound waves for longer-range transmission (up to 300m at 1 Mbps), suitable for SD-quality video (e.g., 480p streaming) in shallow areas despite noise from shrimp or reefs.[2][6][8]

lines.tagged_text: <null>### Implementation for Portable ROVs</null>

lines.raw_text: - **Commercial Solutions**: Hydromea's Exray integrates LUMA for plug-and-play wireless HD video—check their site for portable kits: [Hydromea](https://hydromea.com). VideoRay offers tethered ROVs but pairs with wireless mods.[9]

lines.raw_text: **Optical communication** is the primary alternative to acoustic modems for high data rate underwater transmission, offering significantly higher bandwidth but limited to short ranges due to water clarity and attenuation.[1][3][5]

lines.raw_text: ### Key Comparison of Underwater Communication Methods
lines.tagged_text: <null>### Key Comparison of Underwater Communication Methods</null>

spans.text: ### Key Comparison of Underwater Communication Methods

lines.raw_text: Acoustic modems provide **medium data rates** (typically 10-60 kbit/s) over **long ranges** (up to several km), making them standard for general-purpose use like ROV/AUV control and telemetry.[1][2][4] For scenarios needing much higher speeds (e.g., 100 kbit/s to Mb/s), optical systems excel in **short-range, high-bandwidth** applications such as close-proximity data transfer between vehicles or docking stations.[1][3][5]

lines.raw_text: ### Additional Context and Options
lines.tagged_text: <null>### Additional Context and Options</null>

lines.raw_text: - **Optical details**: Uses light waves (e.g., blue-green LEDs/lasers) for rates up to 260 kbit/s over 200 m in experiments or 100 Mb/s at short distances, far exceeding commercial acoustic modems' 15-62 kbit/s limits.[2][5] Systems like Sonardyne's BlueComm or custom LED modems from Penguin ASI achieve this but require clear water.[5]

lines.raw_text: For simple visual checks underwater, several lighter alternatives to heavy work-class ROVs are available, including **lightweight inspection ROVs**, **observation-class ROVs**, and **mini ROVs**[5][6][7].

lines.raw_text: ## Lightweight Inspection ROVs
lines.tagged_text: <null>## Lightweight Inspection ROVs</null>

lines.raw_text: **Lightweight inspection ROVs** are specifically designed for high-quality visual inspections in various underwater environments[5]. These vehicles offer several advantages:
lines.tagged_text: <null>**Lightweight inspection ROVs** are specifically designed for high-quality visual inspections in various underwater environments[5]. These vehicles offer several advantages:</null>

spans.text: **Lightweight inspection ROVs** are specifically designed for high-quality visual inspections in various underwater environments[5]. These vehicles offer several advantages:

lines.raw_text: - Easy operation requiring just a two-person team for setup and deployment[5]
lines.tagged_text: <null>- Easy operation requiring just a </null><StatisticalUse>two-person</StatisticalUse><null> team for setup and deployment[5]</null>

lines.raw_text: - Advanced imaging technology with 4K cameras and LED lighting for low-light conditions[5]

spans.text: cameras and LED lighting for low-light conditions[5]

lines.tagged_text: <null>- Long battery life suitable for extended subsea operations[5]</null>

spans.text: - Long battery life suitable for extended subsea operations[5]

lines.raw_text: - Ideal applications include offshore inspections, aquaculture, dam inspections, and search and rescue operations[5]

lines.raw_text: ## Observation-Class ROVs
lines.tagged_text: <null>## Observation-Class ROVs</null>

lines.raw_text: **Observation-class ROVs** are compact vehicles with fewer size constraints than mini ROVs but lighter than work-class options[6]. They can accommodate various sensors including video cameras, side-scan sonar, and multibeam sonar for visual and environmental assessments[6].

lines.raw_text: ## Light Work Class ROVs
lines.tagged_text: <null>## Light Work Class ROVs</null>

lines.raw_text: **Light Work Class ROVs** are smaller and more maneuverable than standard work-class vehicles, designed specifically for simple inspection tasks[2]. Key features include:

lines.raw_text: ## Remote Visual Inspection Tools
lines.tagged_text: <null>## Remote Visual Inspection Tools</null>

lines.raw_text: Beyond ROVs, **borescopes and videoscopes** provide cost-effective alternatives for confined space inspections[1][4]. Semi-flexible borescopes, in particular, offer a good balance between flexibility and image quality at lower costs[4].
